tracker_summary.inventor: Jennifer Doudna, Emmanuelle Charpentier (key 2012 paper); Francisco Mojica (foundational discovery); Feng Zhang (first mammalian cell application)
tracker_summary.current_use: Treating sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia (FDA-approved Casgevy, 2023); cancer immunotherapy; agricultural bioengineering; diagnostics
tracker_summary.initial_funding_source: Japanese Ministry of Education (Ishino, 1987); Spanish Ministry of Education / University of Alicante (Mojica, 1990s)
tracker_summary.initial_funding_reason: Basic microbiology research into E. coli DNA replication; ecology of salt-tolerant microorganisms in Mediterranean saltpans

the_x: Curiosity-driven funding for obscure microbial ecology and bacterial genetics with no envisioned application
the_y: A programmable molecular scissors capable of rewriting the human genome, yielding the first curative gene therapies for inherited blood disorders and a 2020 Nobel Prize
summary: In 1987 a Japanese microbiologist noticed something strange in an E. coli gene sequence. A Spanish graduate student later spent years wading through salt marshes near Alicante studying microbes no one cared about, and noticed the same pattern. His papers were rejected for years. He coined the acronym CRISPR and slowly pieced together that bacteria were running a sophisticated adaptive immune system. Yogurt-company scientists proved it. Then Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier realized the bacterial machinery could be reprogrammed as a universal genetic cut-and-paste tool. In 2023 the FDA approved the first CRISPR therapy — 36 years after Ishino's footnote.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Sequencing E. coli; flagged anomalous clustered repeats in a footnote.

chain.where: University of Alicante; Santa Pola salt marshes
chain.what_they_were_doing: PhD student found identical repeats in saltpan archaea; spent a decade piecing together their function.
chain.the_jump: In October 2003, BLAST revealed spacer sequences matched viral DNA — bacteria were storing genetic mugshots of past infections.
chain.funding: Spanish Ministry of Education; Spanish MCYT grant BMC2000-1385.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Protecting yogurt cultures from phages; showed survivors acquired new spacers matching the attacker.
chain.the_jump: Definitive experimental proof — published in Science from a corporate dairy lab — that CRISPR is a heritable adaptive immune system.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Engineered a single guide RNA; showed Cas9 could cut any chosen DNA target.
chain.the_jump: Leap from 'bacteria use this to fight viruses' to 'a 20-letter RNA sequence can cut any DNA in any organism.' CRISPR became a tool.
chain.funding: HHMI; NIH; Gates Foundation; Swedish Research Council.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Applied CRISPR-Cas9 in mammalian cells; filed the eukaryotic-use patent.
chain.the_jump: Crossed the threshold from bacterial biochemistry to therapeutic relevance — and triggered the billion-dollar patent war.
chain.funding: NIH Director's Pioneer Award; Broad Institute; HHMI.

chain.where: Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen
chain.what_they_were_doing: Secretly implanted CRISPR-edited embryos; announced twin girls with CCR5 edits via YouTube.
chain.the_jump: NEAR-DEATH MOMENT: Threatened global research bans. Doudna's swift moratorium call preserved public trust enough to keep clinical programs alive.
chain.funding: Southern University of Science and Technology.

chain.who: Nobel Committee; Vertex / CRISPR Therapeutics; Victoria Gray
chain.where: Stockholm; clinical sites across USA and Europe
chain.what_they_were_doing: Doudna and Charpentier won the 2020 Nobel Prize; FDA approved Casgevy on December 8, 2023.
chain.the_jump: A bacterial footnote became a Nobel Prize and an FDA-approved cure for sickle cell disease — 36 years later.
chain.funding: Nobel Foundation; Vertex Pharmaceuticals; NIH NHLBI.

sources: Barrangou R, et al. (2007). Science, 315(5819):1709–1712. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1138140
Deltcheva E, et al. (2011). Nature, 471:602–607. https://doi.org/10.1038/nature09886
Jinek M, et al. (2012). Science, 337(6096):816–821. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1225829
Cong L, et al. (2013). Science, 339(6121):819–823. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1231143
Isaacson W. (2021). The Code Breaker. Simon & Schuster.
Mojica FJM. (2016). FEMS Microbiol Letters. https://doi.org/10.1093/femsle/fnw056
